title: NVIDIA's Blackwell Ramp: What the Hyperscaler Capex Numbers Are Actually Saying
excerpt: The four largest cloud providers just guided to another step-up in 2026 capex. We translated that into NVIDIA-relevant dollars and what it means for the next four quarters.

children.text: Across Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, the aggregate capex guide for 2026 stepped up roughly 18% above where consensus was sitting going into the prints. Almost all of that delta is AI infrastructure: GPUs, networking, power, and the data centers to house them.

For NVIDIA, the number that matters isn't total capex — it's the AI-attached portion. Our rough mapping puts that at well above $300B in aggregate next year, and the published methodology of 'GPU dollars per AI capex dollar' implies NVIDIA can hold its share of that wallet through Blackwell Ultra.

The pushback is fair: customer concentration is real, ASIC roadmaps are accelerating, and the 'ROI on AI capex' question hasn't been definitively answered. We just think it gets answered slower than the bears want.

title: Shopify's Margin Story Is Real — Here's the Numbers That Matter
excerpt: Three quarters into the post-logistics era, the operating leverage is finally showing up. We pulled the GMV vs. opex line and the picture is the cleanest it has looked in years.

children.text: Shopify's free cash flow margin has expanded ~600 bps over the last twelve months while GMV continued to compound. The combination of a leaner cost base post-logistics divestiture and durable take-rate gains from Shop Pay and Capital is starting to read in the actual numbers, not just the narrative.
Plus and B2B traction tells us whether Shopify can keep pushing into mid-market merchants without giving up the simplicity that made it the default for indie commerce. So far the answer is yes.

title: Canadian Banks 2026: Why RY Stays the Anchor and Where We're Watching for Cracks
excerpt: Provisions are up, NIM has peaked, and housing is still the obvious risk. We compare the Big Six on the metrics that actually matter for the dividend.

children.text: The Canadian banks have collectively moved past peak NIM, and provisions for credit losses are rising as borrower stress in mortgages and commercial real estate filters through. The headline doesn't change the long-term thesis, but it changes the near-term setup.
Why RBC is still our preferred name

Diversification across capital markets, wealth, and the HSBC Canada acquisition gives RBC a structurally lower-volatility earnings stream than the more domestic-retail-heavy peers. The wealth management arm in particular is doing more growth lifting than people realize.

title: The GLP-1 Supercycle, Explained: How Mounjaro and Wegovy Rewrote the Pharma Map
excerpt: What GLP-1 drugs actually do, why supply has been the bottleneck, and the pipeline behind the headline names that will shape the next leg.

children.text: GLP-1 receptor agonists are not new — but the dosing, formulation, and outcomes data of tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound) and semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy) crossed a threshold that turned 'a diabetes drug' into 'a generational consumer health franchise.'
Why supply, not demand, has been the bottleneck

These are sterile injectables manufactured in highly specialized fill-finish plants. Capacity expansion takes years, regulatory inspections, and capital. Lilly and Novo are both racing to build it — that race is the actual story.

title: After the May FOMC: What Powell's Tone Shift Means for Stocks
excerpt: The dot plot didn't move much but the press conference did. We translate Powell's shift in framing into what it usually means for risk assets, fixed income, and the dollar.

children.text: The May statement was nearly unchanged. The press conference wasn't. Powell's framing on the labor market shifted enough that markets are now pricing one additional cut by year-end and a softer dollar path.

For equities, the typical playbook from this kind of dovish pivot is duration-friendly: long-duration tech outperforms cyclicals at the margin, and high-quality dividend payers tend to catch a bid from rate-sensitive flows.
